stories.title: The Bone-Thief's Burden
stories.text: Corvhen moved through Keshtir's Lampwick Quarter as ash fell like snow—consequence of the Remaking, which had turned gold to dust and certainty to scattered fragments. She carried bone-picks her grandmother had carved, tools that sang faintly when locked mechanisms resisted. The Dominion wanted her. All bone-carvers were now prey, suspected of smuggling consciousness-fragments to the Counter-Archive through carved reliquaries that seemed empty but hummed with stolen memory. She descended seven floors beneath the administrative heart, past auditors who never looked up from their phantom-ledgers—documents that rewrote themselves hourly, contradicting yesterday's orders. The vault before her was sealed with meteoric iron and bone-resonance, a mechanism Gareth the Locksmith had forged before disappearing. She'd known him. He'd taught her that locks were conversations: you listened to what they wanted to become. Her picks sang. The lock listened. Inside: not consciousness-fragments but something worse. A ledger written in blood-ink, documenting three versions of the Dominion's authority—each claiming to be the true state, each issuing contradictory tax-levies. The fractured reality of the Remaking had splintered even tyranny itself. Corvhen photographed nothing.

stories.title: The Merchant's Third Choice
stories.text:
At the Wailing Scar—where three timelines bled into each other like watercolors—Torvin the spice-merchant was dying of choices. He carried a sealed flask of pre-Remaking water, the only thing keeping the Seventeen Transformed Merchants from dissolving into pure starlight. They waited in the Drowned Cathedrals, caught between flesh and transcendence, and Torvin held their anchor. A demon-broker appeared as he counted his coins—all of them now worthless except the consciousness-fragments, which sang in his pockets like caged birds. The broker wore a woman's face but smelled of sulfur and contracts. "Give me the water," she said. "The Seventeen wish to complete their ascension. You delay them from mercy."

"Mercy," Torvin repeated, bitter. "They'll dissolve. They'll stop being anything at all."

"Exactly. Suffering ends."

He considered it. In timeline A, he had already given her the water—the Seventeen were gone, transformed into something the next world would remember as myth. In timeline B, he had refused—they lingered, neither alive nor dead, their consciousness fragmenting across months of agony.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Divided Self
stories.text: Merenna existed in three versions simultaneously, and each was drowning. Original Merenna guarded the Sapphire Deep's living coral platforms, where the Tear of the Drowned God was visibly failing—thin as parchment, glowing sickly green. The boundary-seal that had held for a thousand years was collapsing within days, maybe hours. Around her, pearl-keepers wailed the old funeral songs. Mirror Merenna swam the midnight trenches far below, negotiating with abyssal entities—things older than the drowned god itself. They offered a renewal covenant: one willing sacrifice per moon-cycle, eternally, to keep the Tear sealed. Blood-price forever. This Merenna had already agreed, signing in her own luminous blood. Echo Merenna rode a merchant galley on the surface, meeting with bone-singers who promised a new way: no blood. Instead, daily choice. Every dawn, the deepkin would renew the covenant by choosing community, harmony, shared memory. It required trust. It required believing people could choose mercy without compulsion. The three versions wept in synchrony—a harmonic grief that cracked pearl-shells in the vaults. They were fragments of one consciousness, and they could not agree.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Bone-Carver
stories.text:
Naela descended the Ironspine foothills, where Skarath the fragment-hoarder had carved a lair from living stone. The wyvern had ceased hunting consciousness-fragments weeks ago—an unthinkable act for a creature whose immortal hunger had defined three centuries. Now she was dying, her gold-hoard turned to dust, her hoarding-instinct inverted. She wanted to *give*, and it was burning her alive. Naela carried bone-carving tools and a simple proposal: let me teach you the Elder Tongue. Let me show you how to carve meaning instead of accumulating it. Skarath lay coiled around empty stone, her eyes scaled with cataracts of grief. "I have nothing left," the wyvern rasped. "No gold. No treasure. What could I possibly carve?"

"Memory," Naela said. She took the wyvern's talon—each claw was a weapon that could cleave mountains—and placed it against prepared bone. "Teach me your oldest recollection. Not as hoard. As gift."

Skarath trembled. Three centuries of hoarding had created a callus around her heart; giving was surgery without anesthetic. But she spoke.

stories.title: The Shepherd's Refusal
stories.text:
Kestran tended his flocks in the High Meadows of the Steppe Marches—land that had not yet fractured into competing versions of itself. The Remaking's chaos stayed distant here, beyond the wind-scoured peaks. His shepherd's crook was carved from thorn-wood and bound with singing-bone, a post-Remaking tool that hummed when predators approached. One evening, a fae-touched scout appeared at his fire. She wore feathers and antlers and smelled of deep forests that did not exist on Valdris anymore. "The Twilight Court sends word," she said. "A wyvern-lord shelters refugees in the Ironspine—one of your kind has arrived carrying consciousness-fragments we seek. The Court offers reward: safe passage to lands beyond the Remaking's reach. Choose us, and you keep your flock. Refuse, and we'll take them as price."

Kestran did not move. "I shelter no refugees. I tend sheep."

"You *will* shelter them," the fae said, and her voice carried compulsion. "The Court has spoken."

But the shepherd's crook sang. Not in warning—in refusal.

stories.title: The Bone-Carver's Burden
stories.text:
In the hidden catacomb north of Pelimor, Valren carved bone-flutes while the world above crumbled. The Remaking had shattered everything—gold dust blew through abandoned streets, consciousness-fragments screamed silently in glass jars, and the Dominion's auditors hunted bone-singers like rats in grain-stores. Valren worked by bioluminescent moss, his fingers remembering what his mind refused: the Elder Tongue syllables that could bind or free a scattered soul. His grandmother's tools hung above his workbench—copper picks worn smooth by sixty years of theft and craft. She had stolen for tyrants once, locked doors that ought to open. Then she learned bone-singing and spent her last decade carving flutes that refused to stay locked. "Choose what you make," she'd whispered before the Luminous Ascension took her name. "Not all prisons are worth holding."

A refugee child crawled into the catacomb that evening—a girl no older than eight, her shadow scattered across three timelines, her eyes too knowing. She carried a consciousness-fragment in a cloth sack: her mother's laughter, stolen by auditors and sold to a Dominion collector in Keshtir.

stories.title: The Merchant's Threshold Gambit
stories.text: Raen had walked the Wailing Scar three times and lived to count her gold—or what passed for gold now, threadbare and worthless. The Scar was where three timelines intersected like fault-lines in glass, and if you timed your step correctly, you could trade with merchants who existed in different moments of the world's collapse. A bolt of pre-Remaking silk in one timeline became fresh water in another. A wyvern's scale-token paid for passage in a third reality where dragons still honored old debts. Today she carried something rarer: a vial of pre-Ascension starlight, sealed in amber by a bone-singer who'd paid with her eyes to see what the old stars looked like. It was worth a fortune if she could cross to the Obsidian Tower's timeline—but that crossing took more than timing. It took a covenant. She found the Threshold-Keeper where three markets overlapped, a figure neither young nor old, made of glass and possibility. "I want safe passage," Raen said. "Not for myself. For the refugees caught between timelines.

stories.title: The Warlord's Last Stand
stories.text: Kessara's father had ruled the Crimson Vale for thirty years through fear and phantom-gold. The Remaking erased both in a single night. By the time Kessara inherited his war-tent, Soroth was nothing but a broken man counting dust in a sealed vault, waiting for a currency that would never spend again. But his daughter had learned something he never would: the war could not be won with gold. It had to be won with stories. Kessara opened the Crimson Vale's gates—not from mercy, but from mathematics. Bodies meant labor. Labor meant trade goods. Trade goods meant power that didn't depend on the weight of metal. She gathered bone-singers from the ruins, locksmiths from collapsed cities, cartographers who'd burned their own eyes rather than finish prophecy. They taught her people to survive the Remaking's aftermath. But her father still held the vault. Malachar's gold lay beneath the capital, three hundred pieces sealed by blood-oath and older magics. Soroth had killed for that hoard; he would kill again to guard it. Kessara walked into the vault alone.

stories.title: The Tide-Singer's Choice
stories.text: Captain Sera had ridden the Sapphire Sea for seventeen winters, and the merfolk had never once tried to drown her. But that was before the Tear of the Drowned God began to fail. The boundary between human and deepkin realms was cracking. In seven days—perhaps fewer—the abyss would break the seal. The deepkin leadership had fractured into three warring versions of themselves, each proposing a different covenant: blood-sacrifice (the old way), daily-choice renewal (the bone-singers' offer), or a marriage-pact that would bind surface and abyss in mutual oath. Sera's crew had picked her a mate from the deepkin's embassy: Krell, a warrior whose shadow sang in frequencies no human ear should hear. He was beautiful in the way deep-water creatures are beautiful—dangerous, strange, and luminous with pressure. She loved him. She also knew he would die if the Tear shattered—not from drowning, but from the cosmic wound opening beneath. His realm would flood, and he would be swept away into the void between worlds.

stories.title: The Glass-Forger's Rebellion
stories.text: Thyra had worked in Keshtir's furnace-houses for twenty years, singing consciousness-fragments into glass blades that killed without question. The Dominion auditors paid well in the days of gold; after the Remaking, they paid in threats and ration-reductions. By midsummer, she was hiding her apprentices in the cellars of the Glimmerstone Quarter, teaching them a song the Dominion did not know. The rebellion had no generals, only songs. Each glass-forger in Keshtir hummed a different frequency—counter-melodies that unmade the Dominion's phantom-ledgers when they sang them aloud. Thyra had discovered the pattern by accident: consciousness-fragments, when heard correctly, contained not just memories but refusals. They could be instructed to reject consolidation, to scatter themselves further rather than submit to auditor control. But the Dominion had begun executing glass-forgers. Three this week alone, hung from the Lampwick Quarter's old towers as warning to others. Thyra's youngest apprentice, a girl named Sella, had been caught trying to smuggle fragments out of the furnace-house.
